coredata.dc:title: Target-oriented synthesis of yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for intelligent and real-time visual detection of malachite green in fish
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xinru
Zhang, Yihao
Gao, Sineng
Xing, Haoming
Wang, Dingfeng
Hu, Houwen
Qian, Xiaoqing
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: As a triphenylmethane dye, malachite green (MG) is strictly regulated in aquaculture by numerous countries due to its persistence and acute toxicity, posing significant risks to human health. This has driven the demand for rapid and sensitive detection methods for MG. Herein, we present a lateral flow test strips (LFTS)-based sensing platform utilizing yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for real-time visual detection of MG. N-CDs are synthesized via a solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and 4-aminostyrene as precursors, exhibiting strong yellow fluorescence. The presence of MG leads to fluorescence quenching of N-CDs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE), achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 79 nM over a linear range of 0.079–40 μM. Additionally, a portable fluorescence sensing device is constructed by integrating the N-CDs, LFTS and smartphone-assisted RGB (red, green, blue) analysis enabling on-site visual detection of MG within a concentration range of 0.133–30 μM, with a LOD of 133 nM. Meanwhile, practical applications in fish tissue samples exhibit satisfactory recoveries ranging from 98.8 % to 105.6 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 2.72 %. These results suggest that this proposed LFTS platform exhibits high accuracy and reliability for MG detection in aquatic products. Therefore, this simple, cost-effective and on-site detection approach provides a promising strategy for ensuring food safety and safeguarding human health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Malachite green
Lateral flow test strips
RGB analysis
Visual on-site detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Materials and reagents, instruments, determination of fluorescence quantum yield, synthesis of N-CDs
Visual detection of MG based on LFTS sensing platform
Fluorescence detection performance of N-CDs for MG
Mechanism for fluorescence sensing toward MG

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-CDs: (a) TEM image. Inset: high-resolution TEM image. (b) Particle size distribution. (c) AFM image and the height distribution. (d) Raman spectrum. (e) FT-IR spectrum. (f) Full XPS spectrum. (g–i) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively.

Optical properties of N-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and PL spectra of N-CDs. Inset: photos of N-CDs solutions under sunlight and UV light irradiation at 365 nm. (b) Emission spectra of N-CDs with increasing excitation wavelengths (400–500 nm). (c) 3D fluorescence scanning pattern of N-CDs. Relative PL intensities of N-CDs as functions of (d) UV light irradiation time at 365 nm, (e) storage time and (f) saline solution concentration.

(a) PL spectra of N-CDs with various concentrations of MG. (b) Correlation between (F
0 –F)/F
0 and MG concentration. (c) Corresponding linear fitting plot. (d) UV–vis absorbance spectra of N-CDs, MG, N-CDs@MG and N-CDs + MG. (e) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, along with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MG. (f) Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs@MG. (g) Schematic representation of N-CDs-based MG sensing.

(a) Illustration of MG detection using LFTS the sensing platform. (b) Design of the LFTS sensing platform. (c) Images of the sensing platform at various MG concentrations. (d) Relationship between the R/R
0 ratio and MG concentration, including linear fitting and correlation analysis. (e) RGB analysis of standard solutions after MG addition. Significant differences with p < 0.01 for comparison between pre- and post-MG samples. (f) Application of the LFTS sensing platform applied to actual sample detection.
Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure of N-CDs (a) and their practical application in on-the-spot MG sensing (b–c).

head.ce:title: Target-oriented synthesis of yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for intelligent and real-time visual detection of malachite green in fish

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a triphenylmethane dye, malachite green (MG) is strictly regulated in aquaculture by numerous countries due to its persistence and acute toxicity, posing significant risks to human health. This has driven the demand for rapid and sensitive detection methods for MG. Herein, we present a lateral flow test strips (LFTS)-based sensing platform utilizing yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for real-time visual detection of MG. N-CDs are synthesized via a solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and 4-aminostyrene as precursors, exhibiting strong yellow fluorescence. The presence of MG leads to fluorescence quenching of N-CDs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE), achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 79 nM over a linear range of 0.079–40 μM. Additionally, a portable fluorescence sensing device is constructed by integrating the N-CDs, LFTS and smartphone-assisted RGB (red, green, blue) analysis enabling on-site visual detection of MG within a concentration range of 0.133–30 μM, with a LOD of 133 nM. Meanwhile, practical applications in fish tissue samples exhibit satisfactory recoveries ranging from 98.8 % to 105.6 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 2.72 %. These results suggest that this proposed LFTS platform exhibits high accuracy and reliability for MG detection in aquatic products. Therefore, this simple, cost-effective and on-site detection approach provides a promising strategy for ensuring food safety and safeguarding human health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aquatic products, such as fish, shrimp and crabs, provide high-quality proteins, unsaturated fatty acids, vitamins and other essential nutrients, making them vital for human health. Malachite green (MG) has been widely utilized in aquaculture and fisheries due to its effectiveness against parasitic and fungal infections [1]. However, due to its persistence and acute toxicity, MG poses significant environmental and health risks, including carcinogenicity and teratogenicity [2,3]. Consequently, the use of MG is strictly regulated in China and banned in many other countries [4]. According to relevant regulation of the European Union, the minimum required performance limit for MG is established at 2 μg/kg [5]. Nevertheless, the illegal use of MG persists due to its low cost and strong antibacterial properties. Thus, developing a reliable and convenient strategy for the visual detection of MG is of significant importance for dietary security and human health.

Current detection methods for MG, such as nanozyme-assisted oxidation strategies, electrochemical sensors, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, mini-column assays and molecular imprinting, offer various advantages but suffer from limitations including high cost, the need for skilled personnel, complex sample preparation, time-consuming analysis and instability [6–10]. Specifically, electrochemical analysis is sensitive, but electrode fouling and matrix interference can limit its effectiveness [11]. Raman spectroscopy is thought to complicate the analyses, making its analytical applications difficult [12]. In contrast, fluorescence-based techniques exhibit notable advantages such as rapid response, simplicity, cost-efficiency and practicability [13,14]. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs), a class of promising photoluminescent nanoparticles, are particularly appealing for sensing applications owing to their exceptional photostability, ease of preparation, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [15]. These features make them especially useful for MG detection [16,17]. For example, Du et al. reported CD-based fluorescence sensors derived from fish scales for detecting MG in water and turbot samples [18]. Singh et al. prepared nitrogen-doped CDs using citric acid and urea as precursors through a hydrothermal reaction, for label-free MG detection [19]. Cheng et al. synthesized a selective and sensitive N-doped CDs fluorescent sensor for MG analysis in environmental water [20]. However, the photoluminescence (PL) of most CDs typically falls within the blue and green regions, which are severely affected by autofluorescence of sample matrix [21]. In contrast, long-wavelength-emission CDs can effectively mitigate autofluorescence interference and enhance anti-interference capability [22]. Therefore, a crucial demand arises for the targeted synthesis of long-wavelength-emission CDs to improve analytical performance. In addition, lateral flow test strips (LFTS) have gained increasing attention due to their user-friendliness, short assay time, low interferences and affordability over the past several years [23]. Advancements in smartphone imaging and processing technologies have made smartphones viable tools for signal readout and quantitative visual analysis [24]. On this basis, it is significant to synthesize target-oriented long-wavelength-emission CDs and further integrate them into a LFTS platform for intelligent and rapid MG detection in food safety applications.

Herein, yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are target-oriented synthesized using o-phenylenediamine and 4-aminostyrene as precursors via a solvothermal method. N-CDs serve as fluorescence probes for the specific detection of MG (Scheme 1 ). The overlap between the absorption spectrum of MG and the excitation/emission spectra of N-CDs enables fluorescence quenching through both fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). A fluorescence-based sensor is constructed to detect MG, achieving satisfactory detection range and low limit of detection. Furthermore, an innovative smartphone-assisted and N-CDs-based LFTS sensing platform is constructed for intelligent and real-time visual monitoring of MG in aquatic products. Therefore, this work not only presents the target-oriented synthesis of yellow fluorescent N-CDs but also provides a novel approach for MG detection, showcasing potential applications in food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and reagents, instruments, determination of fluorescence quantum yield, synthesis of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Details about the materials and reagents, instruments, measurement of fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and the synthesis of N-CDs were shown in the Supporting Information.

MG solutions at various concentrations were mixed with N-CDs (80 ng/μL), and the total volume was adjusted to 3 mL using phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 6). The PL spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 452 nm after incubating at room temperature for 2 min. To assess the selectivity and anti-interference ability of N-CDs, a variety of interfering substances (including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Co2+, Pb2+, Mn4+, Ba2+, Cl−, F−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, alanine (Ala), tryptophan (Try), serine (Ser), cysteine (Cys), proline (Pro), asparaginate (Asn), glycine (Gly), glucose (Glu), dopamine (Dop), indigocarmine (IND), tartrazine (TRZ) and sunset yellow (SY)) were tested with and without MG following a similar experimental procedure.

Two types of fish (perch and crucian) were purchased from a local market near Ningbo University and used as real samples. The preparation of fish tissue samples was conducted according to previously reported methods with slight modifications [25]. After removing bones and skin, 1 g of fish tissue was weighed, homogenized and immersed in 5 mL of acetonitrile. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the supernatant was collected and used as the analytical solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of MG based on LFTS sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The on-the-spot visual sensing platform constructed in this study consisted of a smartphone and LFTS. The LFTS comprised a sample pad, a nitrocellulose (NC) membrane and an absorbent pad. The N-CDs probe was fixed onto the NC membrane as the test line (T-line), while starch was immobilized below the T-line to serve as the control line (C-line). When standard or sample solutions mixed with I2 were dropped on the sample pad, the targete analyte was carried toward the absorbent pad by the inherent capillary effects. A chromogenic reaction at the C-line validated proper test. In the presence of MG, the fluorescence intensity of the T-line varied proportionally with MG concentration under ultraviolet (UV) light. Fluorescence images were captured using a smartphone camera and analyzed with a mobile application (Color Picker) to convert chromatic data into accurate RGB (red, green, blue) values.

Fig. 1 (a) displays the morphological and dimensional characteristics of N-CDs, as determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). A lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, observed in the high-resolution TEM image (inset in Fig. 1(a)), corresponds to the (100) planes of graphitic carbon, suggesting the distinct crystallinity of N-CDs [26]. Fig. 1(b) illustrates that N-CDs possess an almost spherical shape and are evenly dispersed, with particle sizes ranging from 2.5 nm to 6.5 nm and an average diameter of 4.6 nm. As depicted in Fig. 1(c), the atomic force microscope (AFM) result further confirms the spherical shape of N-CDs with an average height of 2.4 nm. The Raman spectrum identifies the presence of disordered and graphitic carbon at the D-band (1382 cm−1) and G-band (1575 cm−1), respectively (Fig. 1(d)) [27]. The intensity ratio (I D/I G) of 0.83 suggests a high degree of crystallinity, consistent with the high-resolution TEM analysis. The broad diffraction peak centered at approximately 23.8° in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern corroborates the graphene-like structure of N-CDs (Fig. S1) [28]. As illustrated in Fig. 1(e), the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum indicates the existence of hydrophilic functional groups on the N-CDs surface, such as N–H/O–H (3375 cm−1), CO (1611 cm−1) and C–O (1012 cm−1), which contribute to their water solubility. Additional peaks at 1494 cm−1 and 1442 cm−1 correspond to the CC and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively [29,30].

To further evaluate elemental doping and surface functional groups of N-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is carried out. The full XPS spectrum of N-CDs in Fig. 1(f) displays three distinct signals at 284.72 eV (C 1s), 398.69 eV (N 1s) and 531.53 eV (O 1s). As depicted in Fig. S2, the elemental content of C, N and O is determined to be 83.48 %, 11.34 % and 5.18 %, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(g)) confirms the existence of C–C/CC (284.45 eV), C–N (285.5 eV), C–O (285.9 eV) and CN/CO (287.65 eV) [31,32]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum in Fig. 1(h) resolves two peaks corresponding to C–N–C (398.65 eV) and N–H (400.15 eV) [33,34]. Fig. 1(i) presents the high-resolution O 1s spectrum, with peaks at 531.45 eV and 533.45 eV attributed to CO and C–O, respectively [35,36]. The XPS results are in good agreement with the FT-IR spectra, confirming the existence of oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CDs.

The photoluminescence properties of N-CDs are investigated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2 (a)). As illustrated in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, an evident absorption band at 289 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ transition in the aromatic domains [37]. Meanwhile, a broad absorption band around 417 nm corresponds to the n-π∗ transition associated with oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups [38]. According to the fluorescence spectra, N-CDs show a prominent emission peak at 561 nm when excited at 452 nm. N-CDs solution appears slight orange under daylight and exhibits yellow fluorescence under UV irradiation (inset in Fig. 2(a)). As shown in Fig. 2(b), N-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission behavior, maintaining a single emission peak across the excitation range of 400–500 nm, which is further confirmed by the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence scanning pattern in Fig. 2(c). Furthermore, the QY of the emission at 561 nm is measured to be 21.7 %.

The stability of N-CDs under various conditions is essential for their applications. Fig. 2(d) shows the relative PL intensity of N-CDs after exposure to UV light for different times intervals, indicating robust photostability. Additionally, as presented in the bar statistical graph (Fig. 2(e)), the fluorescence intensity remains stable after storage for one month, with only a slight reduction in intensity. Fig. 2(f) demonstrates that N-CDs maintain PL stability even in a high-concentration NaCl solution (2 mol/L), indicating excellent salt tolerance. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S3(a), the N-CDs maintain consistent fluorescence across a wide pH range of 4–11. The N-CDs display satisfactory thermal stability, sustaining consistent performance throughout the temperature range from 15 °C to 65 °C (Fig. S3(b)). In summary, the robust fluorescence stability of N-CDs under diverse conditions lays a strong groundwork for their potential applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection performance of N-CDs for MG
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the detection performance, several critical factors, including pH, N-CDs concentration and reaction time, are thoroughly investigated. Specifically, Fig. S4(a) illustrates that the relative fluorescence intensity decreases progressively as the pH varies from 4 to 6, reaching a minimum at pH = 6, and then increases from 6 to 10. This observation indicates that MG exerts the most pronounced fluorescence quenching effect on N-CDs at pH = 6, corresponding to the highest detection sensitivity for MG. Consequently, the pH = 6 is selected as the optimal condition for MG detection. Similarly, the relative fluorescence intensity of N-CDs toward MG initially declines and subsequently rises as the N-CDs concentration increases (Fig. S4(b)). At a concentration of 80 ng/μL, N-CDs exhibit the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency, making this concentration ideal for further experiments. Additionally, the optimal incubation duration is determined, which is essential for rapid analysis. Fig. S4(c) indicates that the fluorescence intensity quenches rapidly within the first 2 min and then stabilizes. Therefore, a pH of 6, a N-CDs concentration of 80 ng/μL and an incubation time of 2 min are selected for subsequent determinations.

To access the selectivity of the probe for MG detection, the sensing performance of N-CDs is explored against various ions and amino acids, including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Co2+, Pb2+, Mn4+, Ba2+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, F−, Ala, Try, Ser, Cys, Pro, Asn, Gly, Glu, Dop, IND, TRZ and SY. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs remains unaffected by other interferents at equivalent concentrations, except for dyes (MG, IND, TRZ and SY), indicating remarkable selectivity and anti-interference capability (Fig. S5). Therefore, the linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and the hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) classification methods are used to distinguish dyes mixtures in complex detection situation. The results show that the dyes at three concentrations are correctly classified into distinct clusters, respectively, with no misclassification or overlap (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, whether it is a binary or ternary mixture, N-CDs exhibits excellent performance to classify them into their respective categories. Therefore, N-CDs demonstrate applicability in detecting MG along with associated dyes and possess the capability to discriminate them by LDA and HCA methods. As exhibited in Fig. 3 (a), the emission spectra of N-CDs show notable changes in response to increasing MG concentrations (0–250 μM). Fig. S9 presents that the yellow fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreases as MG concentration increases. The quantitative data of (F
0 –F)/F
0 (where F and F
0 denote the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs with and without MG, respectively) are plotted for MG concentrations ranging from 0 to 250 μM (Fig. 3(b)). Fig. 3(c) illustrates a strong linear correlation between MG concentration and the fluorescent signal ratio ((F
0 –F)/F
0 ) within the range of 0.079–40 μM. The LOD is calculated to be 79 nM using formula 3σ/k, in which σ signifies the standard deviation of the blank solution, and k signifies the slope of the calibration curve. Compared with existing approaches of MG detection, the N-CDs-based probe developed in this study offers a wider detection range and superior LOD (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for fluorescence sensing toward MG
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3(d) presents the UV–vis absorbance spectra of N-CDs, MG, N-CDs/MG mixed solution, and the theoretical sum of the individual spectra of N-CDs and MG. The absence of peak shifts of the theoretical sum (N-CDs + MG), compared with the N-CDs/MG mixed solution (N-CDs@MG) suggests that no static covalent compounds or new chemical bonds are formed between N-CDs and MG. Furthermore, Fig. 3(e) displays the PLE and PL spectra of N-CDs, along with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MG. Notably, the MG absorption band significantly overlaps with the excitation and emission peaks of N-CDs, indicating that fluorescence quenching results from both IFE and FRET mechanisms [39]. Subsequently, the Zeta potentials of the N-CDs and MG are measured as −10.74 mV and +20.17 mV, respectively (Fig. S10), further confirming the occurrence of electrostatic attraction between them. The interaction strength between N-CDs and MG, quantified by the binding constant (K), is estimated using the following equation [40]:
(1)
1 /
(
F −
F 0
)
= 1 /
(
K
(
F s
−
F 0
)
C
)
+ 1 /
(
F s
−
F 0
)
where F, F
0 , F
s and C denote the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at a specific MG concentration, in the absence of MG, at saturated MG concentration and the target compound content, respectively. Fig. S11 illustrates that K between N-CDs and MG is 1.03 × 103 mol−1, indicating a robust binding affinity. Recent studies indicate that the FRET is facilitated by the spectral overlap and spatial proximity between donor and acceptor, supporting the conclusion that FRET serves as the dominant mechanism responsible for the reduced luminescence in N-CDs [41,42]. Further evidence for FRET is provided by Fig. 3(f), where the fluorescence decay lifetime of N-CDs is significantly reduced upon MG addition, from 2.08 to 1.77 ns, indicating energy transfer between N-CDs and MG [43].

Moreover, to substantiate the existence of IFE, Parker Equation (2) and Equation (3) are applied [44]:
(2)
I cor
/
I obsd
= 2.3 l
A ex
/
(
1 −
10
− l
A ex
)
×
10
h
A em
× 2.3 d
A em
/
(
1 −
10
− d
A em
)
(3)
E = 1 − I /
I 0
where I
cor and I
obsd denote the corrected and observed fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 561 nm under 452 nm excitation, respectively. A
ex and A
em denote the absorbance values at the excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. The cuvette-specific parameters l, h and d are the cuvette width, the distance from the excitation beam edge to the cuvette edge and the thickness of excitation beam, respectively. E represents the quenching efficiency. E
obsd and E
cor denote the observed and corrected quenching efficiencies, which are calculated from I
obsd and I
cor . As detailed in Table S2, E
obsd and E
cor (i.e., with IFE removed) both increase with increasing MG concentration, reaching 31.4 % and 20.1 %, respectively, thereby confirming the occurrence of IFE. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is attributed to be the synergistic effects of FRET and IFE, as depicted schematically in Fig. 3(g).

In recent years, LFTS have emerged as a prevailing and rapid method for target detection. Meanwhile, smartphone imaging technology has made significant advances in identifying color and brightness changes, enabling the development of compact and intelligent detection systems. Therefore, a LFTS sensing platform integrated with smartphone imaging is developed for the rapid and visual detection of MG. The overall sensing process for MG detection is portrayed in Fig. 4 (a), leveraging N-CDs/LFTS with smartphone imaging. Upon the addition of MG standard solution onto the sample pad, capillary forces drive it toward the absorbent pad (Fig. 4(b)). During the lateral flow process, the analyte first reaches the T-line, then continues to the C-line. A chromogenic reaction at the C-line (changing from colorless to blue) guarantees the validity of the test. The yellow fluorescence quenching of N-CDs results from the synergistic effects of FRET and IFE between N-CDs and MG. Fig. 4(c) illustrates detection of MG standard solutions at various concentrations using the LFTS sensing platform. The fluorescence intensity at the T-line decreases progressively as MG concentration increases. Furthermore, RGB values at the T-line are extracted via a smartphone application. As shown in Fig. 4(d), a linear correlation between RGB parameters and MG concentration is established. The R/R
0 value decreases upon the introduction of MG. Within the concentration range of 0.133–30 μM, a linear relationship is observed and represented as R/R0 = −0.01054 MG+1.0056 (R2 = 0.994), with a LOD of 133 nM. The quenching effect of MG on N-CDs is substantiated through RGB analysis. R/R
0 values decrease significantly with increasing MG concentrations, compared to those without MG, as depicted in Fig. 4(e). These findings suggest that the LFTS sensing platform is a promising tool for visual and on-site MG detection.

To assess the feasibility and accuracy of the LFTS-based sensing platform, MG detection in actual samples, such as perch and crucian, is carried out using the standard addition method (Fig. 4(f)). Video S1 records the test procedure and reveals notable color changes at the T-line under UV light after the addition of real samples. As summarized in Table S3, the recovery rates of spiked MG range from 98.8 % to 105.6 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 2.72 %, indicating high precision in aquatic products detection. Additionally, HPLC is chosen as the reference method, and its results are consistent with those obtained from the proposed method. In addition, taking actual storage environments into consideration, we conduct a 15-day testing period for LFTS under different temperature. During each testing session, three groups of samples are randomly selected and 50 μM solution of MG is added, the RGB value of T-line on LFTS is monitored at different temperature. As shown in Fig. S12, the R/R
0 value exhibits no significant variation over time at different temperatures, indicating that the LFTS demonstrates excellent temperature adaptability and shelf stability. Therefore, the LFTS sensing platform offers a reliable approach for monitoring MG in actual samples, providing a crucial tool for food safety assurance.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2025.344319

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

To sum up, N-CDs with yellow emission are synthesized via a solvothermal reaction using OPD and 4-aminostyrene. The introduction of MG leads to a substantial decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, attributed to the synergistic effects of FRET and IFE. Meanwhile, N-CDs show a linear response to MG in the concentration range of 0.079–40 μM, with a low LOD of 79 nM. Additionally, an innovative LFTS sensing platform based on the N-CDs probe and smartphone-based RGB analysis is designed for MG detection. This platform exhibits a linear detection range of 0.133–30 μM and a LOD of 133 nM. The constructed LFTS-based sensor features low cost, effectiveness and user-friendliness, making it a viable approach for real-time MG detection. Ultimately, the successful detection of MG in actual samples demonstrates the outstanding practical utility and performance of the proposed method. This work not only synthesizes target-oriented yellow fluorescent N-CDs successfully but also provides a novel, intelligent and real-time visual detection strategy for MG, with promising applications in dietary security and environmental monitoring.

Xinru Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yihao Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sineng Gao: Supervision, Software, Methodology. Haoming Xing: Validation, Supervision, Data curation. Dingfeng Wang: Visualization, Supervision, Investigation. Houwen Hu: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaoqing Qian: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Data curation. Da Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61604084).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced removal of malachite green using calcium-functionalized magnetic biochar
Int. J. Environ. Res. Publ. Health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wang, W. Chen, R. Zhang, Y. Xing, Enhanced removal of malachite green using calcium-functionalized magnetic biochar, Int. J. Environ. Res. Public. Health 19 (2022) 3247. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19063247.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of G-quadruplex/thioflavin T fluorescent probe for label-free detection of malachite green
Microchem. J.
